data.title: Catalyst Watch: Eyes on post-Iran economic data, Fedspeak, and cybersecurity
data.description:
Welcome to Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch - a breakdown of some of next week's actionable events that stand out. Read more about the events that may impact stock prices next week.

 

Monday - March 23

  • Volatility watch - Coinbase (COIN) and Lululemon (LULU) have a high level of implied volatility based on options trading. Sunrun (RUN) and Omega Therapeutics (OMGAQ) head into the new week with a high level of short interest outstanding on them. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Tilly’s (TLYS), Adecoagro (AGRO), and Kore Group (KORE). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Crane Harbor Acquisition (CHAC), Lifecore Biomedical (LFCR), and HNI (HNI).
  • Energy watch - Oil prices are likely to remain volatile next week, with levels heavily dependent on Middle East headlines, shipping developments, and supply reports.
  • Fedspeak - Federal Reserve speakers next week include Vice Chair for Supervision Michael Barr, Governor Adriana Kugler, Governor Philip Jefferson, San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, and Philadelphia Fed President Anna Paulson, along with additional remarks by Governor Stephen Miran.
  • Dividend watch - Companies that have an ex-dividend date coming next week include Broadcom (AVGO), Altria (MO), Seagate (STX), and Dick’s Sporting Goods (DKS).
  • IPO watch - No new IPOs are expected to price during the week and begin trading. The IPO lockup period expires for certain blocks of shares of Megan Holdings (MGN). The analyst quiet period ends on Generate Biomedicines (GENB) to free up analysts to post ratings.
  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include WeRide (WRD).
  • All day - Climb Global Solutions (CLMB) will begin trading on a split-adjusted basis following the four-for-one stock split.

 

Tuesday - March 24

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include GameStop (GME), Smithfield Foods (SFD), and Braze (BRZE).
  • All day - Shareholders with Two Harbors Investment (TWO) will vote on the deal to be acquired by mortgage wholesaler UWM Holdings (UWMC).
  • All day - JFB Construction (JFB) will begin trading on a split-adjusted basis following the two-for-one stock split.
  • All day - Flash PMIs for economies around the world will give investors a chance to assess the economic impact of the Middle East conflict.
  • All day - The ShopTalk conference in Las Vegas will include a keynote from Reddit (RDDT) CEO Steve Huffman. Top execs from Wayfair (W), Stitch Fix (SFIX), Pinterest (PINS), Gap (GAP), Dutch Bros (BROS), and SharkNinja (SN) will also appear.
  • All day - MercadoLibre (MELI) will appear at a Morgan Stanley conference.
  • 9:00 a.m. Zillow Group (ZG) will host an investor event focused on how the company continues to deliver and lead on AI innovation in real estate.
  • 10:00 a.m. The New Home Sales report will be released by the Census Bureau.
  • 1:50 p.m. CrowdStrike (CRWD) CEO George Kurtz will give a keynote address at the RSA security conference.
  • 2:00 p.m. Asure Software's (ASUR) CEO will participate in a panel discussion on AI and Implications for Business Services and Software at the Roth Conference.
  • 4:30 p.m. The Nasdaq short interest report will be released.

 

Wednesday - March 25

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include PDD (PDD), Paychex (PAYX), Chewy (CHWY), Winnebago (WGO), and Jefferies (JEF). Options trading implies a double-digit share price swing for Noodles (NDLS) and Widepoint (WYY) after their reports.
  • All day - Dana (DAN) will hold a Capital Markets Day event in New York City centered on its long‑term "Dana 2030" value‑creation strategy, targeting roughly $10B of annual sales by 2030, margin expansion, and improved free cash flow.
  • All day - Generac Holdings (GNRC) will hold its Investor Day event.
  • All day - Starbucks (SBUX) will hold its annual meeting. One of the items up for a vote is a proposal for an independent board chair by separating the roles of chair and CEO and adopting an independent chair policy.
  • 2:00 p.m. Varonis (VRNS) CEO Yaki Faitelson will give a keynote address at the RSA security conference.

 

Thursday - March 26

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Commercial Metals Company (CMC). 
  • M&A votes - Shareholders with Heritage Commerce (HTBK) and CVB Financial (CVBF) vote on their all-stock deal valued at about $811M. Shareholders with ON24 (ONTF) will vote on the buyout deal from Cvent.
  • All day - The Federal Reserve's Senior Loan Officer Opinion Survey on Bank Lending Practices will be released. The SLOOS report will provide an update on the conditions, supply, and demand of bank loans extended to customers in the U.S. Tightening standards can signal stress in the banking system or broader economy, impacting financial stocks.
  • All day - G7 foreign ministers will meet to discuss issues including the Iran war.
  • 9:00 a.m. Marex Group (MRX) will hold its Investor Day event.
  • 2:00 p.m. Genius Sports Limited (GENI) will host a strategic NewFront event as part of the official IAB NewFronts week. The NewFront will also feature conversations with leaders from Publicis Sports, NBCUniversal (CMCSA), the NFL and WNBA, Lowe's (LOW), and others, exploring how live sports is evolving into a real-time, data-powered, and addressable media environment.

 

Friday - March 27

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Carnival (CCL).

 

Seeking Alpha Editor Josh Fineman contributed to this story.

data.title: Air Products upgraded at J.P. Morgan on helium price recovery
data.description:
Air Products and Chemicals (APD) down 1.1% in Friday's trading despite receiving an upgrade from J.P. Morgan to Overweight from Neutral with a $310 price target, raised from $280, citing the reversal in helium prices due to the closing of the Strait of Hormuz and attacks on the Qatari Ras Laffan energy complex, which is responsible for ~30% of global helium production.

J.P. Morgan's Jeffrey Zekauskas said he expects Air Products (APD) to be a better relative share price performer in an economic environment where the risks of economic deceleration, higher inflation, and upward interest rate movements have increased because of the stability of its earnings growth, adding that half of the company's revenue comes from onsite operations where higher energy costs are passed through to customers.

Air Products (APD) likely will see improved utilization rates among its chemicals and refinery customers in North America given their relative cost advantages, the analyst said; 20%-25% of the company's total revenues stem from chemicals end markets, 20%-25% from refining, and 15%-20% from electronics.

data.title: Russell 2000 enters correction territory, and the Dow, S&P 500, and Nasdaq are not far off
data.description:
U.S. equities extended their decline on Friday, with small-cap stocks leading the downturn as rising yields continue to pressure risk assets. The Russell 2000 Index (RTY)(IWM), a widely followed benchmark for U.S. small-cap companies, has officially entered correction territory, falling 10.3% from its January 22 closing high. 

The index was last trading near 2,435, hovering just below its 200-day moving average of 2,437—a key technical level closely watched by investors. A sustained break below this threshold could signal further downside momentum for the small-cap segment, which is often more sensitive to borrowing costs and economic shifts. 

The broader market has also shown signs of strain. The tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) has declined 9.8% from its record high, while the Dow (DJI) is down 9.5%, nearing correction territory as well. The S&P 500 (SP500) has pulled back 7% from its peak. 

Much of the recent weakness has been driven by a sharp rise in U.S. Treasury yields, as investors increasingly price in a higher-for-longer interest rate environment. Expectations that the Federal Reserve could maintain elevated rates—or potentially resume rate hikes into 2026—have weighed heavily on equities, particularly growth-oriented and smaller-cap stocks.

Dow ETFs: (DIA), (DDM), (UDOW), (DOG), (DXD), and (SDOW).

S&P 500 ETFs: (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (FXAIX), (VFIAX), (VFFSX), and (SWPPX). 

Nasdaq ETFs: (QQQ), (QQQM), (SQQQ), (TQQQ), (QLD), and (QID).

data.title: Take-Two drops for the seventh straight session
data.description:
Shares of Take-Two Interactive (TTWO) slipped 0.73% to $200.28 in the afternoon trade on Friday, adding to six straight sessions of decline.

The stock has fallen around 3.6% between March 12 and March 19 compared to a 0.99% decline in the S&P 500 during the same period.

The decline came amid announcements from Nvidia at the Game Developers Conference, where the company unveiled a range of artificial intelligence-powered video generation and game development tools, alongside comments from Take-Two Interactive’s CEO Strauss Zelnick, who said using AI to create major titles like Grand Theft Auto would be “laughable,” adding that such technology cannot replace human creativity and engagement in building blockbuster games.

According to Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating system, TTWO is rated a Hold, with a score of 3.32 out of 5, receiving an A+ for growth but an F in terms of valuation.

An analyst at Seeking Alpha upgraded Take-Two Interactive Software to “Neutral,” citing improving bookings prospects and a more balanced risk-reward profile following the recent selloff, while noting that strong growth and a robust pipeline, including Grand Theft Auto VI, support the outlook.

The analyst added that competitive pressures remain, pointing out that “AI is also leveling the playing field, enabling creatives and designers without technical chops to also create their own games at a fraction of the cost of a studio.”

While on the Wall Street, 26 of 28 analysts rate the stock a buy or higher, one recommends a hold, and one of them rates it with a sell or lower.

Shares have gained around 0.14% in the past month but remain down about 23% year-to-date.

data.title: Palantir's Ontology likely to remain 'competitive advantage,' MS says
data.description:
Palantir Technology's (PLTR) Ontology product is likely to remain a “competitive advantage” over the competition, given the enterprise software's decades of experience, Morgan Stanley said.

Ontology, which Palantir uses to map raw data to real-world objects (such as assets, customers, or transactions) and their relationships, acting as a sort of “digital twin,” Morgan Stanley analyst Sanjit Singh said.

“By bringing together disparate sources of enterprise data (ERP, CRM, databases, sensors, documents, etc.) and mapping them to the real-world entities and events that constitute an organization’s business, relationships and actions among entities and events can be modeled or defined,” Singh wrote in a note to clients. “Importantly, the core elements of the Ontology (objects and properties) are linked directly to the relevant enterprise data sources which are continuously updated. Consequently, building applications, automations, and agents becomes much easier once a thoroughly defined Ontology is in place.”

Singh, who has an Equal-Weight rating and $205 price target on Palantir, said the benefits of Ontology are numerous, including that it offers “connectivity at scale” by putting information that a large company can use into one common business language. Secondly, it also improves interpretability by allowing workers to work in familiar business terms instead of jargon or tables they may not be familiar with.

It also creates economies of scale, as new apps and workflows can be built right on top of Ontology, and it allows for decisions to be captured and recorded across a company. Lastly, it has an artificial intelligence component, as it “gives models and agents structured, real-time context rather than forcing them to reason over fragmented or poorly organized enterprise data,” Singh said.

And since Palantir has been working on Ontology for years, it may be difficult for competitors to replicate, Singh posited. 

“While we have tried to simplify the underlying concepts behind the Ontology model, building and implementing an Ontology in a large enterprise is a laborious process that requires coordination between Palantir forward deployed engineers (in some cases many of them) and subject matter/domain experts at the customer organization,” Singh explained. “Proficiency requires deep industry, domain and operational knowledge as well as strong aptitude for design. Our expert conversations note that while Ontology Manager is a software capability in the Foundry platform, simply uploading a data schema or attempting to use an AI engine to automate Ontology development without deep semantic understanding of the business will lead to operational failure. Furthermore, the accumulated knowledge from decades of experience building and activating ontologies in the most demanding environments across multiple industries and domains, best positions Palantir as the company mostly likely to succeed in accelerating Ontology deployment leveraging AI, resulting in quicker time to value for customers and faster revenue realization for Palantir.”

data.title: The bottom 60% of Americans are worse off than they were two years ago
data.description:
The bottom 60% of American households are financially worse off than they were two years ago, a situation that could spell disaster for Republicans in the upcoming midterm elections, according to Meredith Whitney, CEO of Meredith Whitney Advisory Group.

“It is clear for the bottom 60% of households that they’re worse off than they were two years ago,” Whitney said in an interview with CNBC, adding that these voters originally supported Trump as a referendum on their economic conditions four years prior.

Whitney pointed to alarming signs that lower-income Americans are “barely living paycheck to paycheck.”

She highlighted the explosive growth in earned wage access programs, where employees pay fees to receive their wages early rather than waiting for their regular paychecks.

“If you work at Walmart and you can’t wait two weeks to get your paycheck, you’ll pay a fee to access the pay that you’ve gotten for three days,” Whitney explained, calling it “not a good sign” for consumer health.

The financial strain on these households is compounded by a significant pullback in subprime lending, which has cut off traditional credit access for many Americans.

Whitney noted that this leaves consumers with “zero wiggle room” when facing financial emergencies. As a result, they are forced to seek out more expensive forms of liquidity, creating a cycle that puts even greater strain on already stretched budgets.

This economic reality could translate into significant political consequences, Whitney warned. “Unless the Republicans can pull a rabbit out of the hat, this is going to be a very big referendum against them in the midterms,” she said, predicting a potential “major, major landslide” defeat for the party.

Whitney also raised concerns about the stability of the current administration, suggesting that officials may begin leaving before the midterms if they sense a significant electoral defeat is coming. She expressed worry about the “unpredictability of what comes out of this administration when backed into a corner.”

The potential political fallout could threaten existing policy progress, Whitney cautioned. She warned that positive developments from the current administration could face repeal or subpoena if power shifts, describing the prospect of such political instability as “scary.”

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

More on U.S. politics

  • Lawmakers urge SEC chief to restrict Chinese companies' access to U.S. capital market - report
  • Trump shows support for continuing Powell investigation
  • Fed chair staying on as governor would defy norms, Bessent sasy
  • Powell’s future at the Fed is in focus as prediction markets price in rising exit odds
  • Lack of clear off-ramp clouds prospects for near-term 'TACO' climbdown, Craig Shapiro says

data.title: Peacock sees a surge of new subscribers after the Super Bowl and Olympics
data.description:
Peacock saw a surge in new subscribers during February, as was largely expected, with the streaming service at the center of the sports world. The streamer added an estimated 4.9M new subscribers in February to mark its biggest month ever, driven by the Winter Olympics, Super Bowl LX, and the NBA All-Star Game. 

A market research report from Antenna indicates that Peacock captured about 34% of all new premium streaming sign-ups that month. Separate data from Ampere indicated that the service generated roughly 968,000 new sign-ups on Super Bowl Sunday alone, with a third of those new customers selecting the higher-priced Premium Plus tier. 

Notably, the results reflect NBCUniversal's (CMCSA) strategy to engineer a “Legendary February” to reignite growth after Peacock’s subscriber base had plateaued near 41M for much of 2025. App analytics from Apptopia suggest that Peacock retained about 16.5% of new Olympic-driven subscribers two weeks after the Olympics, and time spent on the platform by the new users nearly tripled as Peacock leaned on new originals and NBA coverage.

data.title: Delinquencies hold steady, charge-offs rise: February Credit Pulse
data.description:
U.S. credit card delinquencies held steady month-over-month across major banks in February, while charge-offs shot up, according to Seeking Alpha's February Credit Pulse.

Seeking Alpha tracks delinquency and charge-off numbers for seven banks in its monthly roundup: American Express (AXP), Synchrony Financial (SYF), Citigroup (C), Bread Financial (BFH), Capital One (COF), JPMorgan Chase (JPM), and Bank of America (BAC).

Average delinquency rate in February remained unchanged month-over-month at 2.81%. However, the metric remained above the year-ago level of 2.59%. The pre-pandemic February 2020 average was 2.93%.

Average net charge-off rate for the month came in at 3.83%, far above the 3.53% rate seen in January and the pre-pandemic level of 3.75%. However, charge-offs remained below the February 2025 level of 4.39%.

"Credit card volumes fell sharply—a typical seasonal trend as consumers paid down holiday balances," said the February edition of the Federal Reserve's Beige Book report released on March 4.

Consumer sentiment was seen deteriorating in March on the back of the Iran conflict.

The University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index fell to 55.5 in March's preliminary reading, down from 56.6 recorded in February and 56.2 consensus, according to a report released on March 13. The index hit its lowest level of the year.

"Interviews completed prior to the military action in Iran showed an improvement in sentiment from last month, but lower readings seen during the nine days thereafter completely erased those initial gains," said Surveys of Consumers Director Joanne Hsu.

Here is a look at the February delinquencies and net charge-off rates:

data.title: Potential for recession is at 30%, but macroeconomic developments should be taken seriously – Morgan Stanley’s Lisa Shalett
data.description:
Lisa Shalett, chief investment officer at Morgan Stanley Wealth Management, believes the potential for a recession remains relatively low despite mounting economic pressures.

In an interview with CNBC, Shalett acknowledged that “the potential for an actual recession or an actual bear market that goes with it is still less than 30%,” though she cautioned that investors need to take current macroeconomic developments seriously.

The shift in global central bank policy has been dramatic, creating significant headwinds for markets. Shalett pointed to what she called a “radical repricing in global bond markets,” noting that the six largest global central banks have pivoted from expecting nearly 600 basis points of cumulative rate cuts to potentially hiking rates by as much as 500 basis points.

This transformation matters significantly for the global cost of capital, company multiples, and overall market valuations.

Shalett expressed concern about the potential economic fallout if the ongoing conflict continues to drag on. She warned that supply chain pressures would likely begin affecting corporate margin expectations first and, if prolonged, could lead to “demand destruction and a more stagflationary type outlook.”

The strategist also cautioned against complacent investor behavior in the current environment. She argued that the mentality of “FOMO; buy the dip, every crisis is an opportunity, is dangerous thinking,” noting that the market has created extreme and sometimes illogical price gaps, particularly between highly valued semiconductors (SMH), (SOXX), (SOXL) and shunned software (XSW), (IGV), (IGPT) stocks.

Despite the challenges, Shalett sees significant opportunity in the financial sector (XLF), particularly among globally systematically important banks. She pointed to upcoming regulatory reforms, including changes to Basel endgame and GSIB surcharges, which could release $250B-$275B that can be put to work in terms of share repurchases, additional deal underwriting, or direct lending.

Overall, while macro headwinds persist, Shalett believes the current market environment offers selective opportunities for investors willing to look beyond the surface. She sees potential for extreme trades to reverse themselves, creating compelling positions on both the long and short sides of the market.

data.title: Trump says U.S. aims to prevent Iran from rebuilding its military
data.description:
President Donald Trump said the U.S. is aiming to inflict enough damage on Iran so that it can never rebuild its military.

In an interview with MS Now, Trump said that while the U.S. could end its campaign "right now," Iran would still be able to rebuild its military over time, a scenario he deemed as not "acceptable."

“If we left right now, it would take them at least 10 years to rebuild, but rebuild they will,” Trump told MS Now, according to CNBC. “If we stay longer, they’ll never rebuild."

Trump also told MS Now that the U.S. and Israel share “largely similar” objectives for Iran, with the “big difference” being that Israel lives "right next door."
